docs.title: RemSim
docs.text: RemSim is a modular FRC physics library for simulation, analysis, and robotics workflow integration.

docs.title: Key Capabilities (implementaion ongoing)
docs.text:
• Unified Physics Engine:
  • Rigid body, drivetrain, and mechanism simulation
  • Realistic field, collision, and sensor modeling
  • Multi-language Bindings:
  • Native C++ core
  • Python and Java bindings for rapid prototyping and integration
  • Simulation Runtime:
  • Plug-and-play robot code simulation
  • Sensor pipelines and WPILib bridge
  • CAD Import:
  • Load URDF, STL, and more for custom robots and fields
  • Extensible & Modular:
  • Add your own models, forces, and plugins

docs.title: Project Goals
docs.text:
• Accuracy: Realistic, physically-based simulation for FRC robots and game elements
  • Integration: Seamless workflow from CAD to code to simulation
  • Accessibility: Usable by teams of all experience levels, with Python/Java/C++ support
  • Open Source: Community-driven, MIT-licensed

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• API Usage: How to use the library in your code
  • Integrators: Numerical integration methods used by the physics engine
  • Vector3: 3D vector operations and helpers
  • Quaternion: 3D orientation and rotation utilities
  • Architecture: System design and extensibility
  • Physics Reference: Underlying models and equations

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# C++
# See examples/cpp/minimal_world.cpp

# Python
pip install frcsim-physics
python examples/python/simple_world_demo.py

# Java
# See examples/java/ShooterPredictionExample.java

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
We welcome issues, pull requests, and ideas from the FRC and robotics community.

Simulate. Innovate. Win.

docs.title: API Usage
docs.text: This page describes the primary usage patterns for RemSim in C++, Python, and Java.

docs.title: C++
docs.text:
Use the C++ examples as the reference entry point:

// See examples/cpp/minimal_world.cpp for a complete runnable setup.

Typical flow:

  • Create a physics world.
  • Add rigid bodies and configure mass/inertia.
  • Apply forces (gravity, springs, motors, or custom force generators).
  • Step the world using a fixed timestep.

docs.title: Python
docs.text:
pip install frcsim-physics
python examples/python/simple_world_demo.py

Typical flow:

  • Import bindings and create a simulation world.
  • Configure bodies and environment.
  • Step simulation in a loop and read back state.

docs.title: Java
docs.text:
Use the Java example as the reference entry point:

// See examples/java/ShooterPredictionExample.java for usage.

Typical flow:

  • Create physics objects through Java bindings.
  • Configure system parameters.
  • Step simulation and consume state for robot logic.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
• Use a fixed timestep for deterministic behavior.
  • Keep units consistent (SI recommended).
  • Validate with tests before tuning coefficients.
  • Prefer explicit force models over ad-hoc state edits.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:
• Architecture
  • Physics Reference
  • Integrators

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: RemSim is organized as a modular monorepo with clear separation between core physics, bindings, runtime applications, and documentation.

docs.title: High-Level Structure
docs.text:
• core/physics-core/: C++ physics engine implementation.
  • core/bindings-python/: Python bindings.
  • core/bindings-java/: Java bindings.
  • core/gamepiece-models/: Gamepiece models for simulation.
  • apps/sim-runtime/: Runtime integration for robot simulation workflows.
  • apps/viewer-plugin/: Visualization and timeline tooling.
  • examples/: Language-specific examples.
  • vendordep/tests/: Unit and integration tests.

docs.title: Physics Core Design
docs.text:
Key components include:

  • Math primitives: vectors, quaternions, matrices, and integrators.
  • Rigid-body modeling and assemblies.
  • Force generators and aerodynamic models.
  • Physics world orchestration.

The architecture is designed to support extension through additional force models, body types, and runtime integrations.

docs.title: Build and Test
docs.text:
• CMake drives native builds.
  • CTest executes test binaries.
  • Helper scripts streamline configure/build/test workflows.

docs.title: Documentation Strategy
docs.text: Documentation is authored in Markdown under mkdocs/docs/ and published with MkDocs Material.

docs.title: Related Pages

docs.title: Integrators (frcsim::Integrator)
docs.text: This page documents the integrators provided in the frcsim physics library, which are used for updating positions, velocities, and orientations in physics simulations.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Integrator struct provides static methods for several common numerical integration techniques used in real-time physics engines. These methods are designed for use with the library's Vector3 and Quaternion types.

docs.title: 1. integrateLinear
docs.text:
Type: Semi-Implicit Euler (a.k.a. Symplectic Euler)

  • Usage:
  • integrateLinear(position, velocity, acceleration, dt)
  • Description:
  • Updates velocity and position using the semi-implicit Euler method, which is stable and widely used in real-time physics.

  • $$ \text{velocity} \mathrel{+}= \text{acceleration} \times dt \ \text{position} \mathrel{+}= \text{velocity} \times dt $$

docs.title: 2. integrateLinearExplicit
docs.text:
Type: Explicit Euler

  • Usage:
  • integrateLinearExplicit(position, velocity, acceleration, dt)
  • Description:
  • Updates position and velocity using the explicit Euler method. Less stable than semi-implicit Euler, but useful for debugging or predictor steps.

  • $$ \text{position} \mathrel{+}= \text{velocity} \times dt \ \text{velocity} \mathrel{+}= \text{acceleration} \times dt $$

docs.title: 3. integrateAngular
docs.text:
Type: Quaternion Integration

  • Usage:
  • integrateAngular(orientation, angularVelocity, dt)
  • Description:
  • Integrates orientation using angular velocity and quaternion algebra.
  • Quaternion derivative: $$ \dot{q} = 0.5 \cdot \omega_{quat} \cdot q $$ where $\omega_{quat} = (0, \omega_x, \omega_y, \omega_z)$

docs.title: 4. integrateAngularVelocity
docs.text:
Type: Angular Velocity Update

  • Usage:
  • integrateAngularVelocity(angularVelocity, angularAcceleration, dt)
  • Description:
  • Updates angular velocity using angular acceleration.

  • $$ \omega \mathrel{+}= \alpha \times dt $$

docs.title: 5. integrateLinearRK2
docs.text:
Type: Runge-Kutta 2nd Order (Midpoint)

  • Usage:
  • integrateLinearRK2(position, velocity, acceleration, dt)
  • Description:
  • Uses the midpoint (RK2) method for more accurate integration, useful for projectiles or high-speed mechanisms.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Vector3
  • Quaternion
  • Physics Reference

This documentation was generated for the file: core/physics-core/include/frcsim/math/integrators.hpp

docs.title: Physics Reference
docs.text: This page summarizes the physical models currently represented in RemSim.

docs.title: Rigid-Body Dynamics
docs.text:
Core rigid-body updates are based on Newton-Euler mechanics:

$$ \mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\alpha} $$

Linear and angular state are integrated per timestep using methods documented in Integrators.

docs.title: Rotation Representation
docs.text:
Orientations are represented with quaternions to avoid gimbal lock and improve numerical stability.

Quaternion state updates follow:

$$ \dot{q} = \frac{1}{2}\,\omega_q\,q $$

See Quaternion for API details.

docs.title: Forces
docs.text:
RemSim supports force models including:

  • Gravity
  • Springs
  • Motors/actuators
  • Aerodynamic terms (drag, Magnus effect)

docs.title: Numerical Considerations
docs.text:
• Use fixed timestep simulation where possible.
  • Select integration methods based on stability and performance requirements.
  • Validate material and damping parameters against expected physical behavior.

docs.title: Current Scope
docs.text: The engine is under active development. Some advanced subsystems are still being expanded.

docs.title: Related Pages
docs.text:

  • Architecture
  • Integrators

docs.title: Quaternion (frcsim::Quaternion)
docs.text: This page documents the Quaternion struct in the frcsim physics library, which represents a quaternion for 3D rotations and provides operations for orientation, interpolation, and vector rotation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Quaternion is a 4D type (w, x, y, z) used for representing 3D rotations. It supports construction from axis-angle, angular velocity, and provides methods for normalization, interpolation, and conversion to rotation matrices.

docs.title: Members
docs.text: • w, x, y, z: The quaternion components (double)

docs.title: Constructors
docs.text:
• Quaternion() — Identity quaternion
  • Quaternion(double w, double x, double y, double z) — Initialize with values
  • Quaternion(double w, Vector3 v) — From scalar and vector part

docs.title: Norms & Normalization
docs.text:
• norm2() — Squared magnitude
  • norm() — Magnitude
  • normalized() — Returns normalized quaternion
  • normalize() — In-place normalization
  • normalizeIfNeeded(eps) — Normalize if not already unit

docs.title: Construction & Conversion
docs.text:
• fromAxisAngle(axis, angleRad) — From axis-angle
  • fromAngularVelocity(omega, dt) — From angular velocity (small angle)
  • toAxisAngle(axis, angleRad) — To axis-angle
  • toMatrix(m) — To 3x3 rotation matrix

docs.title: Interpolation
docs.text: • slerp(a, b, t) — Spherical linear interpolation

docs.title: Operations
docs.text:
• conjugate() — Conjugate
  • inverse() — Inverse
  • operator* — Quaternion multiplication
  • operator+, operator- — Addition, negation
  • operator==, operator!= — Comparison

docs.title: Vector Rotation
docs.text:
• rotate(v) — Rotate a vector
  • forward(), up(), right() — Standard basis directions

docs.title: Utility
docs.text:
• isIdentity(eps) — Check if identity
  • hasNaN() — Check for NaN components
  • operator<< — Output stream

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
frcsim::Quaternion q = frcsim::Quaternion::fromAxisAngle(frcsim::Vector3(0,1,0), M_PI/2);
frcsim::Vector3 v = q.rotate(frcsim::Vector3(1,0,0));

This documentation was generated for the file: physics-core/include/frcsim/math/quaternion.hpp

docs.title: Vector3 (frcsim::Vector3)
docs.text: This page documents the Vector3 struct in the frcsim physics library, which represents a 3D vector and provides a wide range of vector operations for physics simulations.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Vector3 is a 3D vector type with double-precision components (x, y, z). It supports arithmetic, geometric, and physics-related operations, and is used throughout the library for positions, velocities, forces, and more.

docs.title: Members
docs.text: • x, y, z: The vector components (double)

docs.title: Constructors
docs.text:
• Vector3() — Zero vector
  • Vector3(double x, double y, double z) — Initialize with values

docs.title: Arithmetic
docs.text:
• +, -, *, / (with scalars and vectors)
  • Compound assignment: +=, -=, *=, /=

docs.title: Norms & Normalization
docs.text:
• norm2() — Squared magnitude
  • norm() — Magnitude
  • normalized() — Returns normalized vector
  • isZero(eps) — Checks if vector is near zero

docs.title: Dot & Cross Product
docs.text:
• dot(const Vector3&) — Dot product
  • cross(const Vector3&) — Cross product

docs.title: Utility
docs.text:
• clamp(min, max) — Clamp each component
  • lerp(a, b, t) — Linear interpolation
  • distance(a, b) — Distance between vectors
  • projectOnto(axis) — Projection onto another vector
  • reflect(n) — Reflect across a normal
  • torque(r) — Torque at a point

docs.title: Physics Helpers
docs.text:
• magnusForce(velocity, omega, k) — Magnus effect force
  • dragForce(v, Cd, A, rho) — Drag force
  • dynamicGravity(velocity, spin, g, magnusCoeff, gravityEffect) — Gravity with Magnus effect
  • tractionForce(normal, frictionCoeff, normalForce) — Traction/friction force

docs.title: Constants
docs.text:
• zero() — Zero vector
  • unitX(), unitY(), unitZ() — Unit vectors

docs.title: Other
docs.text:
• hasNaN() — Check for NaN components
  • operator[] — Element access
  • operator<< — Output stream

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
frcsim::Vector3 a(1.0, 2.0, 3.0);
frcsim::Vector3 b = a.normalized();
double d = a.dot(b);
frcsim::Vector3 f = frcsim::Vector3::dragForce(a, 0.5, 0.1);
